data.title: Angel Families had a day of remembrance we never should have needed
data.description:
On Feb. 23, 2026, my wife and I stood in the White House for the National Angel Family Day Proclamation. It was a solemn gathering — heavy with grief yet filled with resolve.

President Trump had chosen to establish this day as an acknowledgment of the thousands of Angel Families in our country. These are average, everyday families like our own that had loved ones ripped away from them to dangerous criminal illegal immigrants allowed into the country.

We were grateful to Trump for having the courage to recognize the lives lost and the families forever altered by crimes committed by those who should never have been in this country to begin with.

But as we listened, another feeling crept in alongside the gratitude: disbelief.

We were in utter disbelief of the situation that had been foisted upon us and so many of the other families at this event with us.

No nation should require a day of remembrance for lives lost to totally preventable policy failures. No parent should have to bury his or her child because elected officials placed ideology above enforcement. No family should be forced to navigate unimaginable grief while being told, absurdly, that the system worked as intended.

There was failure at the federal level in securing the border. There was failure at the state level in enforcing the law. There was failure in prioritizing the safety of American citizens. 

And these failures took our sweet Katie’s life. 

Our daughter, Katie Abraham, was killed on Jan. 19, 2025, on the streets of Urbana, Ill. — a sanctuary city in a sanctuary state. She was a passenger in a vehicle struck from behind at nearly 80 miles per hour. First responders had to pry open the car to reach her. Katie died at the scene, and another young woman succumbed to her injuries the next day. Three others were wounded.

The man who hit them fled. The driver, Julio Cucul-Bol, was a previously deported Guatemalan national who had reentered the U.S. in 2022 while President Joe Biden was in office. He had used a Mexican alias and was already known to federal authorities. Yet he was able to live freely in Illinois. Despite prior deportation and despite being known to enforcement agencies, Cucul-Bol obtained a driver’s license. He drove on our roads. He lived in our community. 

He was apprehended in Texas three days later — and just two days after Trump returned to office. We do not know whether Culul-Bol would have been caught under the prior administration. We only know that our daughter would still be alive if existing immigration laws had been enforced before he ever reentered this country. 

While tragedies like these occur far too often, the media remains quiet. And if the media decides to cover stories like Katie’s, it is often with reluctance or deflection. The broader policy context — the predictable consequences of reckless policy decisions — is treated as impolite to mention.

For families like ours, that one-sided narrative only deepens the wound.

Katie was vibrant. She was loved. She was someone’s daughter, someone’s friend, someone with a future. Now she is a statistic in a debate that too many would rather avoid. 

We stood at the White House in her honor — and in honor of all the Angel Families. We appreciated the recognition. But when the ceremony ended and the cameras turned away, our reality remained.

This is a reality compounded by an ongoing federal government shutdown. The very federal agency that is working to prevent policy failures like these is not being funded. Hundreds of thousands of workers are going without pay. Under Trump, our border is the safest it has ever been, yet Democrats continue to view American citizens and their lives as pawns in a political game.

A proclamation cannot restore a life. A speech cannot repair a shattered family. What we ask for is simple: accountability. As Trump said in his State of the Union address, “The first duty of the American government is to protect American citizens, not illegal aliens.” 

He is right. We must become a nation where our law-abiding, taxpaying citizens are valued and protected. We must become a nation where immigrants are welcomed — and assimilated — through our legal system.

That goal will not be easily accomplished. It will require honesty and willingness to confront uncomfortable truths. But achieving it would make the heavy price that Angel Families have paid at least a little more tolerable.

We must find the good that can come from this tragedy and build upon it. But we will never stop asking the question that should haunt every elected official who enabled these policies: How many more Angel Families must be created before we decide that remembrance is not enough?  

Joe Abraham of Illinois is father of Katie Abraham, who was killed in an Urbana, Ill., automobile crash in 2025.

data.title: Kennedy launches new nutrition education medical school partnership: 'This is how we implement the MAHA agenda'
data.description:
Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. on Thursday launched a new partnership with over 50 medical schools across the country on teaching nutrition education.

“Together, with the Department of Education Secretary Linda McMahon, I’m pleased to announce a transformative breakthrough in medical education that will reshape the way that we train doctors in our country and deliver on President Trump’s promise to end the chronic disease epidemic in America,” Kennedy said at a press conference. 

“Fifty-three medical schools across 31 states are driving the national movement to champion nutrition education, reshape the future of medical training. Beginning this fall, these schools will require every medical student to complete 40 hours of comprehensive nutrition education or competency equivalent before graduating,” the secretary added.

“More than 30,000 physicians each year will now graduate equipped with nutrition education to help prevent, treat and reverse chronic disease,” he continued. “This is how we implement the MAHA agenda, this is how we Make America Healthy Again.”

According to a fact sheet on the HHS website, the competency program features 71 requirements for students to reach, including understanding “the principles of a balanced diet according to the new Dietary Guidelines for Americans,” which the White House unveiled in January.

Some parts of the new dietary guidelines have received praise from medical and dietary voices, but there have been questions and critiques about Kennedy’s “upside-down” food pyramid.

The fact sheet also stated that $5 million is being put towards medical and health education programs that incorporate nutrition education in their instruction by HHS.

data.title: Johnson, GOP leaders ask Tony Gonzales to drop reelection after admitting affair
data.description:
House Republican leaders on Thursday called on Rep. Tony Gonzales (R-La.) to end his bid for reelection over an affair that the Texas congressman admitted he had with a staffer in his office who later died by suicide.

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.), House Majority Leader Steve Scalise (R-La.), House Majority Whip Tom Emmer (R-Minn.), and House Republican Conference Chairwoman Lisa McClain (R-Mich.) issued a joint statement urging Gonzales to drop out.

“The Ethics Committee has announced an investigation into Congressman Tony Gonzales’s conduct, and we urge them to act expeditiously. Congressman Gonzales has said he will fully cooperate with the investigation. We have encouraged him to address these very serious allegations directly with his constituents and his colleagues. In the meantime, Leadership has asked Congressman Gonzales to withdraw from his race for re-election,” the House GOP leaders said in a statement.

The statement comes one day after Gonzales admitted in an interview with conservative talk show host Joe Pags that the affair occurred, saying that he “made a mistake” and had a “lapse in judgement.” But Gonzales had resisted calls to resign or end his campaign for reelection.

The House Ethics Committee officially opened an investigation into Gonzales on Wednesday to examine allegations that he violated the House’s code of conduct, which prohibits sexual relationships with staff. 

It opened the investigation the day after Gonzales’s primary in Texas, which sent him to a runoff against a right-wing challenger in May.

It did not open an investigation earlier due to the panel’s rules about taking actions against members within 60 days before an election.

data.title: Paxton offers conditions for potential exit from Texas Senate race
data.description:
Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton (R) suggested Thursday that he would be open to dropping out of the hotly contested Texas Senate primary against Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) if Senate Republicans met several conditions. 

Paxton said on Thursday he would consider exiting the race if Senate leadership eliminated the filibuster — the 60-vote threshold needed to clear most legislation in the upper chamber — and passed a bill that would require voters to show proof of citizenship to register to vote. 

The shift from Paxton comes after President Trump on Wednesday said he plans to endorse in the Senate GOP primary “soon” and called for whoever he doesn’t endorse to drop out of the race.

“John Cornyn is a coward who has refused to support abolishing the filibuster to pass this bill. Now, Fake News reporters and the establishment are trying to destroy me with misinformation,” Paxton wrote on the social media platform X.

“The truth is clear: No one has been more loyal to Donald Trump than me—fighting the stolen 2020 election, being in Mar-a-Lago when he announced his 2024 campaign, and standing with him in NY in the face of lawfare,” he added. “For the good of our country and for the good of passing President Trump’s agenda, I am determined to help him get this done.” 

Cornyn has expressed some openness to changing the filibuster rules — which Trump backed as lawmakers looked to reopen the government during its shutdown last year. However, Cornyn also expressed concern that eliminating the 60-vote threshold rule could be abused by Democrats if they were to return to power.

The incumbent senator has also supported the SAVE America Act, the legislation that would require voters to show proof of citizenship in order to vote in addition to a photo ID requirement to cast a ballot.

The legislation has passed the House but has stalled in the Senate as Republicans don’t have the 60 votes needed to pass the bill. Some Republicans have floated a “talking filibuster,” which would require Democrats to continue talking on the floor in order to halt its passage. The talking filibuster only requires 51 votes in order to pass once Democrats have finished on the floor. 

But Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) has expressed skepticism of a “talking filibuster.” 

Senate GOP leadership has pressed Trump to endorse Cornyn in the May 26 runoff in an effort to avoid an ugly contest against Paxton.

data.title: No peace in Ukraine until Russia returns its children
data.description:
Every serious peace process begins with a test of credibility. Before borders are frozen, guarantees extended, or sanctions eased, negotiators must determine whether the parties are willing and able to comply with the most basic obligations of peace. 

In Ukraine, that test is clear: Will Russia return the Ukrainian children it has abducted?

Secretary of State Marco Rubio recently told a Senate hearing that the territorial status of Ukraine’s Donbas region is the final issue remaining unresolved in the peace negotiations. But that claim gives false hope and ignores one of the issues most important to the American public, across bipartisan lines and championed by a broad coalition of faith communities: the return of Ukraine’s abducted and forcibly assimilated children. 

This is not a marginal humanitarian concern. It is the most direct measure of whether Russia intends to honor any future settlement at all.

Tens of thousands of Ukrainian children remain unaccounted for after being forcibly transferred to the Russian Federation or placed under Russian control. Additionally, an estimated 1.6 million children are living in temporarily occupied territories, where Russia has constructed extensive systems of indoctrination, militarization and legal erasure.

Today, with little humanitarian pressure placed on the Kremlin, Moscow no longer needs to relocate children en masse to sever them from their country. It has institutionalized these practices, embedding them in schools, youth organizations, citizenship laws, and social services.

Yet the 20-point peace framework being developed by Ukraine, the U.S. and allied partners includes only a single brief reference to abducted children and no operational plan for their identification, return or reintegration. This omission is not technical. It reflects an assumption that a reckoning for this crime can be postponed until after an agreement is signed. That assumption is dangerous. 

The return of abducted children is not a maximalist demand. It is a clear, verifiable and morally unambiguous obligation under international law. This issue is also uniquely suited to serve as a confidence-building measure. Unlike territorial disputes or security arrangements, the return of children can be independently monitored and confirmed. Either children are brought home, or they are not. 

Russia’s own behavior underscores why this test matters. Moscow continues to deny that Ukrainian children are being held unlawfully, despite extensive documentation, survivor testimony, and the issuance of International Criminal Court arrest warrants for President Vladimir Putin and Russia’s commissioner for children’s rights, Maria Lvova-Belova. If Russia refuses to acknowledge even the existence of this crime, there is little reason to believe it will comply with more complex or politically costly provisions of a peace agreement.

This is not only a question of trust — it is a question of long-term security. 

Russia’s policies toward Ukrainian children are systematic and strategic. Through forced changes in legal status, reeducation programs, military training, and coerced adoption, the Kremlin is attempting to permanently reshape the identities and loyalties of a generation of Ukrainians.

Ukrainian children are being taught that Ukraine does not exist, that their families abandoned them, and that Russia is their only homeland — a homeland they should be ready to die for.

These children are not being erased from the war. They are being prepared for its continuation.

Any settlement that leaves these systems intact embeds the causes of future conflict into its foundations. A peace agreement that ignores the indoctrination and militarization of children does not resolve the war; it defers it. Worse, it internationalizes the risk, as these practices normalize population transfer and identity-destruction as acceptable tools of statecraft.

There is also a broader precedent at stake. Forced population transfer, particularly of children, is a crime precisely because of its irreversible consequences. If Russia is permitted to retain abducted Ukrainian children without consequence, it signals to other authoritarian regimes that such crimes can be negotiated away.

Peace deals that sidestep massive atrocity crimes against the most vulnerable do not merely fail their victims; they weaken international laws that are intended to protect children worldwide.

Accountability and negotiation are not mutually exclusive. Upholding the law for crimes against children is not an obstacle to peace, but part of its effective long-term architecture. What is incompatible with durable peace is the normalization of child abduction as a bargaining chip.

Finally, any agreement must be sustainable within Ukraine itself. Ukrainians are already being asked to consider painful compromises after years of existential war, in which nearly every person has lost a family member or friend to Russia’s invasion. Abandoning abducted children would make national acceptance of any settlement far more difficult. Bringing captive Ukrainians home is not only a legal and moral imperative.  It is one of the few steps that can help build domestic consent for peace. 

Peace in Ukraine cannot be built on promises alone. It must begin with proof. Until Russia returns the children it has taken — and ends the systems designed to erase those who remain under occupation — no settlement can be considered credible, durable or just. 

Kristina Hook is a nonresident senior fellow at the Atlantic Council and assistant professor of conflict management at Kennesaw State University.

data.title: Rand Paul: Americans 'have been robbed of a public debate' on Iran war
data.description:
Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) rebuked the Trump administration’s rollout of joint military strikes with Israel on Iran in a Fox News opinion piece published Thursday.

Paul cited Congress’s constitutional authority to declare war, saying the decision to go to war “should be a collective decision, with a clear rationale for war articulated.”

“The people have been robbed of a public debate,” Paul wrote. “Let me inform the public that this evasion is intentional.

“The congressional leadership — resigned to their own irrelevance — will gladly hand the president the power to initiate war in exchange for plausible deniability,” the senator continued. “Congressional leaders want to make the case to voters that they are not to be held accountable at the ballot box because they played no role in the decision to go to war. That is not statesmanship. That is shameful.”

Paul was the only senator to vote with Democrats on a war powers resolution on Wednesday. While the resolution carried no penalties, it would have sent a strong message to the Trump administration opposing military action in Iran. 

Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.), who has long advocated for Israel’s right to defend itself, was the only Democrat to vote against the resolution. 

The House of Representatives will hold a similar vote on Thursday afternoon, which is expected to also fail. 

Additionally, Paul criticized President Trump’s announcement of Operation Epic Fury. The president posted a video on Truth Social following the launch of strikes early Saturday morning, in which he shared the news of “major combat operations in Iran.” 

“Americans were not asked if they would bear the burdens of war. Instead, the American people were told, through a presidential eight-minute video posted around 2:30 in the morning, that the country was, once again, at war,” Paul said. 

Paul highlighted the stakes of this conflict, citing the loss of the six U.S. Army Reserve soldiers who were killed during an Iranian counterstrike on a tactical operations center in Kuwait on Sunday. 

“This country is now at war, which has already cost the lives of six American service members, and many more are severely wounded,” he wrote. “Those soldiers and their families deserved a public debate and a vote in Congress before the initiation of hostilities.”

The senator ended his Thursday piece with a plea to his fellow members of Congress.

“No others in our history have been this cavalier with our military men and women and tax dollars as they are at this moment,” Paul wrote. “I urge my colleagues to join me in opposing both this war and the unilateral actions taken without congressional authorization, as the Constitution commands.”

data.title: Blue states challenge Trump's new tariffs
data.description:
Democratic-led states announced Thursday they are suing President Trump over his new tariffs, asserting they are illegal like his previous recently struck down by the Supreme Court.

Following the high court’s 6-3 blockbuster ruling that Trump can’t invoke a 1977 emergency law to impose his sweeping levies, the president has turned his gaze to another law that does authorize tariffs.

Known as Section 122, the president has leveraged it to announce a 15 percent tariff on most imported goods worldwide. 

But the Democratic-led states claim Trump can’t use Section 122, because the statute provides authority limited to respond to a balance-of-payments crisis. 

The case has not yet been docketed, but the states said it would be filed Thursday in the U.S. Court of International Trade. It appears to mark the first major lawsuit challenging Trump’s latest tariffs.

“This is not new terrain for California, but it is exasperating,” California Attorney General Rob Bonta (D) said in a statement. “Why is President Trump — who ran on the promise of making life more affordable for families — breaking the law to raise the cost of living for Americans?” 

Bonta is leading the lawsuit with three other AGs from Democratic-led states: Arizona Attorney General Kris Mayes, New York Attorney General Letitia James and Oregon Attorney General Dan Rayfield. 

They’re joined by Democratic attorneys general in Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Illinois, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, Michigan, Minnesota, Nevada, New Jersey, New Mexico, North Carolina, Rhode Island, Vermont, Virginia, Washington and Wisconsin. 

Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear (D) and Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) also signed on as plaintiffs, as their states’ attorneys general are Republicans. 

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

Trump administration officials have defended the president’s new tariffs, insisting they are legal. The president himself has repeatedly said the Supreme Court’s decision vindicates his move, latching onto comments from the dissenters.

Some of the Democratic-led states were part of the coalition that toppled Trump’s previous tariffs at the high court last month. The businesses they sued alongside, who actually paid import fees, are now leading the fight for refunds.  

“The focus right now should be on paying people back, not doubling down on illegal tariffs,” Rayfield said in a statement. “People are already making hard choices about what to put in their shopping cart. Prices on basics like groceries, clothing and other essentials have all been skyrocketing. At some point, the bills become unmanageable.”

data.title: Trump, Bondi sued over TikTok deal
data.description:
Two investors sued President Trump and Attorney General Pam Bondi on Thursday over the administration’s approval of a deal that saw TikTok spun off into a separate American-owned entity in order to continue operating in the U.S.

Zhaocheng Anthony Tan, a shareholder in Google’s parent company Alphabet, and Garrett Reid, a Meta shareholder, argue the deal violated a law requiring TikTok’s parent company ByteDance to divest from the app or face a U.S. ban.

“For the law to mean something, it must be followed, even—perhaps especially—by the President. Respondents have violated the statute and subverted the will of Congress. Petitioners bring this case to ensure that such violations, and such subversion, do not continue,” the lawsuit, filed by the Public Integrity Project, reads.

TikTok officially spun off its U.S. business in late January. The new entity is majority owned by American investors, with Silver Lake, MGX and Oracle serving as managing investors and each taking a 15 percent stake. ByteDance maintains a 19.9 percent stake.

Oracle is tasked with providing security for the new U.S. TikTok and retraining a copy of the recommendation algorithm on U.S. user data.

“In short, under the announced deal, ByteDance would still control all the essential elements of TikTok,” the lawsuit argues. “Such a deal would subvert the very purpose of the TikTok Law, as ByteDance could continue to push Chinese propaganda and censor the content it does not like, exactly the harm that the law was intended to prevent.”

Tan and Reid contend that, as investors in competing companies, they suffered financially from the deal’s approval, which created a “legal impediment to [their] financial recovery.”

The deal came to fruition a little over a year after the divest-or-ban law was set to take effect. After taking office last January, Trump quickly signed an executive order delaying enforcement of the measure as he sought to reach a deal to keep TikTok available in the U.S.

He pushed back the deadline several more times in 2025, which the lawsuit also argues violated the law. In September, the president announced the administration had reached an agreement for a TikTok spin-off, and he quickly signed an executive order declaring the deal a “qualified divestiture” under the law.

data.title: Congress can help find and rescue tens of thousands of exploited children
data.description:
In March of 2024, I sat before the House Judiciary Committee and told members about 57,000 children.

Fifty-seven thousand boys and girls whose images of sexual abuse were already in the hands of law enforcement. Officers had seen their faces. They had heard their cries. But they did not know their names. They did not know where they were. And they had not yet been rescued.

I told them about Operation Renewed Hope, a global victim identification operation aimed at finding those children that we helped stand up in partnership with Homeland Security Investigations Cyber Crime Center in Fairfax, Va. Each year, 50 to 70 expert analysts and investigators from around the world gather for two full weeks with one singular mission: to study thousands of abusive images and uncover the clues that will lead to children’s locations so they can be safeguarded.

The shadow of a building might narrow a search to a state in the Northeast.

A hotel bedspread might reveal a specific region.

The faint sound of a bird behind a child’s cries once led investigators to a city in the Midwest.

This work is painstaking and emotionally grueling, but it saves lives.

Across three operations, 1,119 children have been tentatively identified and more than 500 have already been safeguarded. These are among the most successful victim identification efforts in the world.

And yet we are barely scratching the surface.

When I testified, we knew there were 57,000 children seen in sexually abusive image series in just one law enforcement database. An image series means the abuse wasn’t isolated. It means the child endured repeated exploitation. It means that somewhere, right now, that child may still be living in what feels like an endless nightmare.

There are half a dozen law enforcement databases around the world. Since 2024, the number of unidentified series has grown to 89,000.

At the Cyber Crimes Center, there are currently seven full-time victim identification analysts assigned to this work — a mere seven people tasked with helping locate tens of thousands of children.

The problem is not a lack of dedication within law enforcement but a lack of resources. These investigators are drowning. No workforce that small can solve a crisis this large.

This crisis is not distant. Experts believe that one-third of the children in the 89,000 unidentified series are here in the U.S. Some of them are infants.

In just the last six months, more than 338,000 unique IP addresses in the U.S. were identified trading child sexual abuse images across peer-to-peer networks. Research shows that 55 to 85 percent of individuals who consume this material also commit hands-on offenses. On average, they have 13 victims each.

The people responsible do not look like monsters. They are often trusted members of our communities — neighbors, coaches, teachers, pastors, even family members. Most child victims are abused by someone in a close position of trust.

Parents play a critical role in protecting their children, but they cannot fight this battle alone. The scale and sophistication of online exploitation have far outpaced what any household can manage.

This is where federal leadership matters. Online and offline exploitation are deeply connected, and the volume of abuse material continues to grow at a staggering pace. The work of identifying a single child requires highly trained analysts who can piece together digital breadcrumbs across jurisdictions before law enforcement can intervene.

Every rescue requires the right people, with the right training, in the right place at the right time. Right now, there simply are not enough of them.

Congress has the power to change that. The bipartisan Renewed Hope Act of 2026 would create and equip a dedicated workforce of 200 analysts, investigators and forensic specialists focused specifically on identifying unknown children seen in sexual abuse images so they can be located and safeguarded.

Two hundred trained professionals is not excessive. It is proportional to the scale of the crisis.

At the Tim Tebow Foundation, we are proud to support congressional leaders working to pass this legislation, because it reflects the scale and seriousness this crisis demands.

For far too long, children have suffered in silence while their abuse has hidden in the darkness and vastness of the internet. That silence must end.

We know these children exist. The question is whether we will build the capacity to find them. The Renewed Hope Act of 2026 is an opportunity to do just that. We have the power — and the moral obligation — to help stop the exploitation, trafficking and abuse of children. Congress must use its authority now.

Tim Tebow is a speaker, entrepreneur, college football analyst for ESPN and SEC Network, the author of six New York Times bestsellers and founder of the Tim Tebow Foundation. He was previously an NFL quarterback, two-time NCAA Football national champion, Heisman Trophy winner and College Football Hall of Fame inductee.

data.title: Rove: Talarico has chance of upset in Texas
data.description:
Republican strategist Karl Rove on Thursday said Texas state Rep. James Talarico (D) has a chance of winning if Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton (R) becomes the GOP nominee.

“[Democrats] haven’t won a Senate race here since 1988, they haven’t won a statewide race since 1994, but everybody believes that this year is the year that if we had a damaged candidate on our ticket that they might be able to pull it off,” Rove said during an appearance on Fox News. 

On Tuesday, Paxton advanced to a May run off for the Republican nomination alongside incumbent Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas).

President Trump has not yet made an endorsement in the race but said one was coming soon. 

The leader asked the candidate he does not back to drop out of the race, which the Texas has Attorney General vowed not to do. 

“That is bad for him. So maybe, maybe that leads me to go the other direction,” Trump told Politico in a phone interview. 

Republican leaders including Senate Majority Leader John Thune (S.D.) have urged the president to endorse Cornyn to avoid a costly campaign for ahead of the November general election.

Rove said Thursday that Paxton has a lot of “baggage” that could prevent him from winning after noting that Democratic primary candidate Rep. Jasmine Crockett (D-Texas) had “no chance whatsoever” at upsetting the GOP nominee in the Senate race.

The longtime strategist said, “Cornyn would win this going away. This is one of the arguments that even the White House political staff has been making.”

“Paxton on the other hand has so much baggage that the Democrats would pour a hundred or two hundred million dollars into the state with the hope that they can pull off something like they did in Alabama when they beat a very troubled Republican candidate and had, briefly, a Democrat senator from the state of Alabama,” he added.
